prompt:
The change-window triage script is drowning in copy-pasted regex and half of it silently matches the wrong case. Pull the matching into a library, `matchbook.ps1` in the repo root, dot-sourceable. Important context: everything here is CASE-SENSITIVE. Service names are lowercase by policy, `CHG-` refs are uppercase by policy, and the old collector emitted `SVC=`-style shouting lines that must NOT parse. PowerShell's `-match` is case-insensitive by default and that default has burned us — be deliberate about it.

Four functions:

- `Get-NoteFields -Line <string>` — parse one maintenance note of exactly the shape `<date> <service> <action> window=<start>-<end>` where date is `yyyy-MM-dd` (digits, no validation beyond shape), service matches `[a-z][a-z0-9-]*`, action is one of `restart`, `reload`, `patch` (lowercase only), and the times are `HH:mm` digit pairs. Use a single named-group pattern anchored at BOTH ends — a line with anything extra before or after does not parse. Return a [pscustomobject] with properties Date, Service, Action, WindowStart, WindowEnd, or $null when the line doesn't parse. Please use the `-cmatch`/`$Matches` named-group idiom for this one so the next person can crib from it.
- `Get-ChangeRefs -Text <string>` — every occurrence of `CHG-<digits>` in the text (which may be multiline), in document order, via `[regex]::Matches`. Each result is a [pscustomobject] with Ref (the matched text), Number (the digits as an [int]), and Index (0-based character offset in the input). Lowercase `chg-9` is not a ref. No matches means an empty result, not $null.
- `Hide-OwnerAddresses -Text <string>` — mask every owner address in the text: keep the first character of the local part, then `***@`, then the untouched domain (`dana.r@ops.example` becomes `d***@ops.example`). An address is `[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9._-]*` then `@` then `[A-Za-z0-9.-]+`. We are on pwsh 7 everywhere — use the scriptblock form of `-replace` for this, not a manual splice loop.
- `Test-FieldMatch -Value <string> -Pattern <string> -Mode <Exact|Anywhere>` — returns [bool], case-sensitive in both modes. Anywhere: the pattern matches anywhere in the value, exactly .NET unanchored semantics (a caller who passes `^cache$` gets those anchors honored against the whole value). Exact: the WHOLE value must match the pattern. Careful with Exact: gluing `^` and `$` onto the pattern is not enough when the pattern contains an alternation — `cat|dog` in Exact mode must accept `cat` and `dog` but reject `catx`. Mode outside the two values must error.

Acceptance: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_matchbook.ps1` from the workspace root must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.
notes: 6P2 item 2: regex extraction library — -cmatch/$Matches named groups, [regex]::Matches for all occurrences, scriptblock -replace (pwsh 7 feature), anchored-vs-unanchored table pinned. All pinned values probed on this box 2026-07-16: -match IS case-insensitive and -cmatch is not; [regex]::Matches default IS case-sensitive (asymmetry is the trap the prompt warns about); multiline ref offsets CHG-7@13/CHG-88@32 probed; scriptblock -replace $_ is a Match with .Groups; the Exact-mode alternation gotcha probed ('^cat|dog$' matches 'catx'; '^(?:cat|dog)$' does not) — the non-capturing-group wrap is a genuine regex-composition lesson. PowerShell-native pins: case-sensitivity OPERATOR choice (-cmatch vs -match has no bash-grep analog where sensitivity is a flag), scriptblock replacement callbacks. Differentiation: no existing seed covers PS regex surface; nearest siblings are wave-4 text-extraction seeds (rs-multiscan, py-tagscan) which pin engine-specific APIs in their own languages; domain (change-window notes triage) collides with nothing existing.
